APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This work sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML interests: end-to-end ASR modeling and training/decoding innovations (joint CTC–attention) evaluated on standard benchmarks (CSJ, HKUST) and compared to strong baselines (hybrid Kaldi, CTC-syllable, LF-MMI TDNN, coverage-term attention). The paper reports hyperparameters and positions itself against relevant prior work, addressing alignment and decoding problems important to the community. From an experimental-rigor perspective I would want code/checkpoints and statistics from multiple runs for full reproducibility, but those omissions do not change that the topic, audience, problem formulation, and evaluation are appropriate for a top venue. | B(5): This paper tackles an active end-to-end ASR problem (joint CTC-attention) that is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues given current interest in sequence models and practical speech recognition. It evaluates on standard, realistic corpora (CSJ and HKUST) and compares against strong baselines (hybrid DNN, CTC, TDNN with LF-MMI, coverage-term attention), and reports hyperparameters and limitations, meeting community expectations for methods and evaluation. The framing — using CTC to mitigate attention alignment failures and avoid linguistic resources — is directly relevant to both ML and speech audiences and is positioned appropriately relative to prior work.

reviewer_a.justification: This work sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML interests: end-to-end ASR modeling and training/decoding innovations (joint CTC–attention) evaluated on standard benchmarks (CSJ, HKUST) and compared to strong baselines (hybrid Kaldi, CTC-syllable, LF-MMI TDNN, coverage-term attention). The paper reports hyperparameters and positions itself against relevant prior work, addressing alignment and decoding problems important to the community. From an experimental-rigor perspective I would want code/checkpoints and statistics from multiple runs for full reproducibility, but those omissions do not change that the topic, audience, problem formulation, and evaluation are appropriate for a top venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an active end-to-end ASR problem (joint CTC-attention) that is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues given current interest in sequence models and practical speech recognition. It evaluates on standard, realistic corpora (CSJ and HKUST) and compares against strong baselines (hybrid DNN, CTC, TDNN with LF-MMI, coverage-term attention), and reports hyperparameters and limitations, meeting community expectations for methods and evaluation. The framing — using CTC to mitigate attention alignment failures and avoid linguistic resources — is directly relevant to both ML and speech audiences and is positioned appropriately relative to prior work.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions (joint CTC‑attention training/decoding to address alignment issues) are stated early. Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear adequate for reproducibility, and the writing quality is described as good with related work coverage sufficient. One area for improvement is clearer reporting of evaluation metrics and more explicit, informative figures/tables or examples to aid readers—these omissions slightly limit clarity but do not prevent understanding. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions—the joint CTC-attention training and decoding—are stated clearly early and motivated by concrete failure modes of attention-only models. Notation and methodological description appear consistent, and related work coverage and reporting of hyperparameters support reproducibility. Minor clarity issues remain: evaluation metrics were not specified in the provided summary and some decoding/experimental details (e.g., exact comparison settings to TDNN/LF-MMI baselines) could be more explicit. Given the generally good writing quality and effective presentation, only small presentation refinements are needed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and the main contributions (joint CTC‑attention training/decoding to address alignment issues) are stated early. Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear adequate for reproducibility, and the writing quality is described as good with related work coverage sufficient. One area for improvement is clearer reporting of evaluation metrics and more explicit, informative figures/tables or examples to aid readers—these omissions slightly limit clarity but do not prevent understanding.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions—the joint CTC-attention training and decoding—are stated clearly early and motivated by concrete failure modes of attention-only models. Notation and methodological description appear consistent, and related work coverage and reporting of hyperparameters support reproducibility. Minor clarity issues remain: evaluation metrics were not specified in the provided summary and some decoding/experimental details (e.g., exact comparison settings to TDNN/LF-MMI baselines) could be more explicit. Given the generally good writing quality and effective presentation, only small presentation refinements are needed.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem—robust end-to-end ASR training and decoding by combining CTC and attention to mitigate alignment-driven insertion/deletion errors—and demonstrates effectiveness on realistic benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) achieving performance comparable to conventional hybrid systems without linguistic resources. From an experimental-rigour perspective the joint CTC–attention idea is likely to change how researchers design training/decoding for sequence models because it directly addresses a key failure mode of attention-only systems and provides a clear, implementable recipe. It falls short of overturning state-of-the-art hybrid methods (did not surpass TDNN+LF-MMI) and reproducibility artifacts (code/checkpoints) aren’t reported in the summary, so its impact is substantial but not transformative. | B(4): This paper tackles an important practical bottleneck for end-to-end ASR—instability and insertion/deletion errors from attention models—by combining CTC alignment constraints with attention training and decoding, which is directly relevant for practitioners seeking lexicon-free systems. Demonstrating comparable performance to conventional hybrid systems on CSJ and HKUST without pronunciation dictionaries suggests strong applied impact across languages and deployment contexts. Although it does not yet surpass state-of-the-art TDNN+LF-MMI results, the approach meaningfully narrows the gap and points to clear follow-up work integrating sequence-discriminative criteria, so it is likely to be adopted and extended by the community.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—robust end-to-end ASR training and decoding by combining CTC and attention to mitigate alignment-driven insertion/deletion errors—and demonstrates effectiveness on realistic benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) achieving performance comparable to conventional hybrid systems without linguistic resources. From an experimental-rigour perspective the joint CTC–attention idea is likely to change how researchers design training/decoding for sequence models because it directly addresses a key failure mode of attention-only systems and provides a clear, implementable recipe. It falls short of overturning state-of-the-art hybrid methods (did not surpass TDNN+LF-MMI) and reproducibility artifacts (code/checkpoints) aren’t reported in the summary, so its impact is substantial but not transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical bottleneck for end-to-end ASR—instability and insertion/deletion errors from attention models—by combining CTC alignment constraints with attention training and decoding, which is directly relevant for practitioners seeking lexicon-free systems. Demonstrating comparable performance to conventional hybrid systems on CSJ and HKUST without pronunciation dictionaries suggests strong applied impact across languages and deployment contexts. Although it does not yet surpass state-of-the-art TDNN+LF-MMI results, the approach meaningfully narrows the gap and points to clear follow-up work integrating sequence-discriminative criteria, so it is likely to be adopted and extended by the community.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The core idea—training an attention-based encoder with an auxiliary CTC objective and using joint decoding—closely matches multiple prior works (e.g., "Joint CTC/attention decoding" and "Hybrid CTC/Attention Architecture" from 2017) and therefore is not a genuinely new method. The paper’s reported contributions appear to be an application of that joint CTC-attention approach to Japanese and Mandarin benchmarks and some language-specific observations (shorter ideogram sequences), which are useful but constitute an incremental extension rather than a non-trivial methodological advance. The authors do compare to strong baselines, but the main technical novelty over existing joint CTC-attention literature is limited, so the contribution would not surprise an expert in end-to-end ASR. | B(2): The core idea—training an attention-based encoder with an auxiliary CTC objective to enforce monotonic alignments and joint decoding—has already been explored in closely related prior work (e.g., the joint CTC/attention papers cited in the retrieval: [1]–[3]). The paper’s application to Japanese and Mandarin and the empirical claim of matching hybrid systems without linguistic resources is a useful empirical contribution but constitutes a mainly incremental, application-level extension rather than a new methodological advance. Overall, the techniques largely combine established methods with limited novelty beyond demonstrating them on specific ideogram languages.

reviewer_a.justification: The core idea—training an attention-based encoder with an auxiliary CTC objective and using joint decoding—closely matches multiple prior works (e.g., "Joint CTC/attention decoding" and "Hybrid CTC/Attention Architecture" from 2017) and therefore is not a genuinely new method. The paper’s reported contributions appear to be an application of that joint CTC-attention approach to Japanese and Mandarin benchmarks and some language-specific observations (shorter ideogram sequences), which are useful but constitute an incremental extension rather than a non-trivial methodological advance. The authors do compare to strong baselines, but the main technical novelty over existing joint CTC-attention literature is limited, so the contribution would not surprise an expert in end-to-end ASR.

reviewer_b.justification: The core idea—training an attention-based encoder with an auxiliary CTC objective to enforce monotonic alignments and joint decoding—has already been explored in closely related prior work (e.g., the joint CTC/attention papers cited in the retrieval: [1]–[3]). The paper’s application to Japanese and Mandarin and the empirical claim of matching hybrid systems without linguistic resources is a useful empirical contribution but constitutes a mainly incremental, application-level extension rather than a new methodological advance. Overall, the techniques largely combine established methods with limited novelty beyond demonstrating them on specific ideogram languages.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates on standard benchmarks (CSJ, HKUST) and compares against strong baselines including Kaldi DNN-hybrid, CTC-syllable, TDNN with LF-MMI and coverage-term attention decoding, which are appropriate and recent for the era. Comparisons appear to be conducted under comparable conditions (same corpora, and the paper explicitly acknowledges that TDNN with LF-MMI still outperforms their method), and hyperparameters are reported. However, the proposed joint CTC–attention approach is very close to contemporaneous work (multiple prior papers on joint CTC/attention exist) and it is not fully clear from the summary whether the experiments include ablations that isolate the contribution of CTC during decoding versus training or whether all directly competing joint-CTC architectures were compared experimentally—hence minor omissions prevent a top score. | B(4): The paper compares to strong and relevant baselines (DNN-hybrid Kaldi recipe, pure CTC, TDNN with lattice-free MMI, and coverage-term attention) on standard corpora (CSJ and HKUST), and it openly acknowledges when a prior method (TDNN LF-MMI) outperforms the proposal. Comparisons appear generally fair in scope, but the summary omits explicit evaluation metric reporting and detailed ablation analyses isolating the contributions of each loss term. Also, given the very close prior work on joint CTC/attention architectures (e.g., joint CTC/attention decoding and hybrid CTC/attention papers), the paper should more clearly position empirical differences versus those specific contemporaneous approaches—hence a minor deduction.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates on standard benchmarks (CSJ, HKUST) and compares against strong baselines including Kaldi DNN-hybrid, CTC-syllable, TDNN with LF-MMI and coverage-term attention decoding, which are appropriate and recent for the era. Comparisons appear to be conducted under comparable conditions (same corpora, and the paper explicitly acknowledges that TDNN with LF-MMI still outperforms their method), and hyperparameters are reported. However, the proposed joint CTC–attention approach is very close to contemporaneous work (multiple prior papers on joint CTC/attention exist) and it is not fully clear from the summary whether the experiments include ablations that isolate the contribution of CTC during decoding versus training or whether all directly competing joint-CTC architectures were compared experimentally—hence minor omissions prevent a top score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to strong and relevant baselines (DNN-hybrid Kaldi recipe, pure CTC, TDNN with lattice-free MMI, and coverage-term attention) on standard corpora (CSJ and HKUST), and it openly acknowledges when a prior method (TDNN LF-MMI) outperforms the proposal. Comparisons appear generally fair in scope, but the summary omits explicit evaluation metric reporting and detailed ablation analyses isolating the contributions of each loss term. Also, given the very close prior work on joint CTC/attention architectures (e.g., joint CTC/attention decoding and hybrid CTC/attention papers), the paper should more clearly position empirical differences versus those specific contemporaneous approaches—hence a minor deduction.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper evaluates the joint CTC-attention approach on two standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) and compares against several reasonable baselines (DNN-hybrid, CTC-syllable, TDNN LF-MMI, coverage-term attention), with hyperparameters reported—so empirical coverage is adequate to show feasibility. However, I see no evidence of critical ablations or sensitivity analyses (e.g., varying CTC loss weight, encoder variants, or decoding strategies) nor of statistical robustness (multiple runs/variance), which are essential to isolate contributions and quantify reliability. Related work is treated adequately and some limitations are acknowledged (did not match TDNN LF-MMI, known CTC/attention trade-offs), but reproducibility is incomplete since code/checkpoints are not mentioned and certain decoding/LM details appear underspecified. Overall the experimental evidence supports the main claim at a baseline level but lacks the deeper analyses and reproducibility artifacts I would expect for full confidence. | B(4): The paper provides solid experimental evidence on two relevant benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) and compares against strong baselines (DNN-hybrid, CTC-syllable, TDNN LF-MMI and coverage-term attention), and it reports hyperparameters and an explicit limitation that it does not yet match LF-MMI TDNN results. However, the summary does not indicate detailed ablation or sensitivity studies (e.g., varying the CTC/attention loss weight, encoder architectures, or decoding strategies) that would clarify which components drive gains, nor is code release mentioned which weakens reproducibility despite hyperparameter reporting. Related work coverage is described as adequate but the existence of very close prior joint CTC/attention papers (e.g., contemporaneous 2017 works) suggests the paper should more clearly position and distinguish its contributions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates the joint CTC-attention approach on two standard benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) and compares against several reasonable baselines (DNN-hybrid, CTC-syllable, TDNN LF-MMI, coverage-term attention), with hyperparameters reported—so empirical coverage is adequate to show feasibility. However, I see no evidence of critical ablations or sensitivity analyses (e.g., varying CTC loss weight, encoder variants, or decoding strategies) nor of statistical robustness (multiple runs/variance), which are essential to isolate contributions and quantify reliability. Related work is treated adequately and some limitations are acknowledged (did not match TDNN LF-MMI, known CTC/attention trade-offs), but reproducibility is incomplete since code/checkpoints are not mentioned and certain decoding/LM details appear underspecified. Overall the experimental evidence supports the main claim at a baseline level but lacks the deeper analyses and reproducibility artifacts I would expect for full confidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides solid experimental evidence on two relevant benchmarks (CSJ and HKUST) and compares against strong baselines (DNN-hybrid, CTC-syllable, TDNN LF-MMI and coverage-term attention), and it reports hyperparameters and an explicit limitation that it does not yet match LF-MMI TDNN results. However, the summary does not indicate detailed ablation or sensitivity studies (e.g., varying the CTC/attention loss weight, encoder architectures, or decoding strategies) that would clarify which components drive gains, nor is code release mentioned which weakens reproducibility despite hyperparameter reporting. Related work coverage is described as adequate but the existence of very close prior joint CTC/attention papers (e.g., contemporaneous 2017 works) suggests the paper should more clearly position and distinguish its contributions.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper states clear empirical claims and evaluates the joint CTC–attention method on two standard corpora (CSJ, HKUST) against reasonable baselines (DNN–hybrid, CTC-syllable, TDNN LF‑MMI, coverage-term attention), and reports hyperparameters, which supports the main experimental claims. However, the summary lacks mention of evaluation metrics, multiple runs or variance/statistical significance, and code/checkpoints for reproducibility, so some experimental rigor is missing. There are no indicated mathematical errors and the methodological idea (multi‑task CTC+attention) is plausible, but the absence of statistical analysis and full decoding/training recipe details prevent a fully watertight validation of the claims. Overall the work is technically sound with minor but important gaps in experimental transparency and reproducibility. | B(4): The paper's main claim—that joint CTC-attention training alleviates attention-model alignment failures and yields competitive results on CSJ and HKUST—is supported by experiments against reasonable baselines (DNN-hybrid, CTC, LF-MMI TDNN, coverage-term attention). Mathematical content appears straightforward (multi-task objective) and hyperparameters are reported. However, the summary omits key experimental details (exact evaluation metrics, statistical significance) and the paper concedes it does not match LF-MMI TDNN performance, leaving some questions about the robustness and generality of the gains across conditions. Overall the technical evidence is solid but not fully complete, so I rate it slightly below flawless.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and evaluates the joint CTC–attention method on two standard corpora (CSJ, HKUST) against reasonable baselines (DNN–hybrid, CTC-syllable, TDNN LF‑MMI, coverage-term attention), and reports hyperparameters, which supports the main experimental claims. However, the summary lacks mention of evaluation metrics, multiple runs or variance/statistical significance, and code/checkpoints for reproducibility, so some experimental rigor is missing. There are no indicated mathematical errors and the methodological idea (multi‑task CTC+attention) is plausible, but the absence of statistical analysis and full decoding/training recipe details prevent a fully watertight validation of the claims. Overall the work is technically sound with minor but important gaps in experimental transparency and reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claim—that joint CTC-attention training alleviates attention-model alignment failures and yields competitive results on CSJ and HKUST—is supported by experiments against reasonable baselines (DNN-hybrid, CTC, LF-MMI TDNN, coverage-term attention). Mathematical content appears straightforward (multi-task objective) and hyperparameters are reported. However, the summary omits key experimental details (exact evaluation metrics, statistical significance) and the paper concedes it does not match LF-MMI TDNN performance, leaving some questions about the robustness and generality of the gains across conditions. Overall the technical evidence is solid but not fully complete, so I rate it slightly below flawless.

main_claims: Proposes a joint CTC-attention end-to-end ASR that leverages benefits of both CTC and attention during training and decoding.
Joint CTC-attention alleviates alignment issues (irregular alignments, insertions/deletions) observed in purely attention-based ASR by using CTC-based alignment as guidance.

Demonstrates the proposed method on Japanese and Mandarin benchmarks and reports comparable performance to conventional hybrid ASR systems without requiring linguistic resources (e.g., pronunciation dictionary, tokenization).
_summary.methodology: Attach a CTC objective to an attention-based encoder network and train/ decode jointly (multi-task/joint CTC-attention) so CTC provides monotonic alignment constraints while the attention decoder models conditional dependencies.
datasets: HKUST Mandarin Chinese conversational telephone speech recognition (MTS)
baselines: TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training (Povey et al.)
Coverage-term based attention decoding (Chorowski and Jaitly, 2016)
limitations: Proposed method did not reach the performance obtained by TDNN with lattice-free sequence discriminative training.

Attention-based ASR suffers from irregular alignments (repetition/insertion and deletion errors) which motivated combining CTC; CTC alone requires language models and graph-based decoding except with extremely large training data.

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Rigour Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior machine learning engineer with a PhD in speech recognition and 8+ years building and evaluating end-to-end ASR systems in both academia and industry.
traits: focuses on experimental design: checks baselines, hyperparameter settings, dataset splits, and reproducibility details
expects ablation studies that isolate the contribution of each component (e.g., CTC loss weight, encoder architecture, decoding strategy)
looks for statistically meaningful comparisons (multiple runs, variance reporting) and clear training/decoding recipes
values implementation details and releaseable artifacts (code, config, model checkpoints) to verify claims
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I evaluate whether the experiments and reported results are sufficient, transparent, and reproducible to support the paper's empirical claims.

reviewer_b.name: Speech Research Impact Analyst
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor specializing in spoken language processing with a strong publication record on sequence-to-sequence and hybrid ASR systems and interest in practical adoption of end-to-end methods across languages.
traits: assesses novelty and positioning relative to prior literature, especially prior joint or hybrid approaches
examines broader impact: applicability across languages, requirements for linguistic resources, and deployment considerations
values theoretical or conceptual clarity about why combining losses addresses specific failure modes (e.g., alignment collapse, insertions/deletions)
notices relevance of evaluation choices (language-specific benchmarks, realistic audio conditions, tokenization) to claimed contributions
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I judge the paper by its novelty, clarity of contribution in the context of prior work, and significance for the ASR community and real-world deployment.
